description: HLLQP - LIFE 03 QZ - Whole Life and Term-100 Insurance - Section 4
examTips: Focus on whole life policy features (guaranteed premium, CSV, non-forfeiture options), T-100 characteristics, participating vs non-participating policies, dividend options, and premium payment structures.

questions.question:
Michael owns a whole life policy that has a face value of $800,000 and a cash value of approximately $100,000. The annual premium on the policy is $8,000, payable until age 100. Michael is considering taking a five-year sabbatical to tour the world and write a thesis.
While he is not concerned about the loss of income or increased expenses during that time, he asks if there is some way he can have relief from paying the premium on his policy. His only stipulation is that he would like to have the same level of coverage for a minimum of five years. He is adamant that if he dies within the next five years, his beneficiary must receive exactly $800,000.
Which of the following options would meet Michael's needs?
options: A. He could surrender his policy.
B. He could have the premiums paid by automatic premium loan (APL).
C. He could choose the extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.
D. He can exercise the reduced paid-up non-forfeiture option.
optionFeedback: Surrendering the policy permanently terminates coverage — the beneficiary would receive nothing upon death.
APL keeps the policy in force, but the death benefit would be $800,000 MINUS the accumulated APL loan balance — not exactly $800,000 as required.
He could choose the extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.
Reduced paid-up insurance provides permanent coverage for a lesser face amount — not exactly $800,000 as required.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Surrendering the policy permanently terminates coverage — the beneficiary would receive nothing upon death; APL keeps the policy in force, but the death benefit would be $800,000 MINUS the accumulated APL loan balance — not exactly $800,000 as required.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: He could choose the extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.

questions.question:
A life insurance policy has an annual premium of $1,000. Given the modal factors below, which statement is correct regarding the required payments?
| Premium Frequency | Modal Factor |
| :--- | :--- |
| Annual | N/A |
| Semi-annual | 0.51 |
| Quarterly | 0.26 |
| Monthly | 0.09 |
options: A. If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay 9% more as compared to paying the premium on an annual basis.
B. If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay $80 more than the stated annual premium.
C. The semi-annual payment would result in the highest total annual premium.
D. The monthly premium would be equal to 1/12 of the annual premium.
optionFeedback: Monthly total = $90 × 12 = $1,080. That is 8% more than $1,000, not 9%.
If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay $80 more than the stated annual premium.
Monthly total ($1,080) is higher than semi-annual total ($510 × 2 = $1,020) — monthly results in the highest total annual premium.
1/12 of $1,000 = $83.33, but the modal factor gives a monthly payment of $90 — these are not equal.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Monthly total = $90 × 12 = $1,080. That is 8% more than $1,000, not 9%; Monthly total ($1,080) is higher than semi-annual total ($510 × 2 = $1,020) — monthly results in the highest total annual premium.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay $80 more than the stated annual premium.

questions.question:
Marcy owns a 30-year limited pay term 100 policy. She is unsure what the word "limited" refers to.
What should you tell her?
options: A. Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would be paid-up and she would retain the coverage.
B. Marcy will be required to pay premiums for a maximum of 30 years. After that, she must convert the policy into a permanent plan or else it will terminate.
C. Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would mature and she would have no more coverage.
D. The coverage will only be in place (limited) for 30 years.
optionFeedback: Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would be paid-up and she would retain the coverage.
A limited pay T-100 does not need to be converted — it is already permanent (T-100). After the payment period, the policy is simply paid-up.
A paid-up T-100 does not mature at the end of the payment period — coverage continues for life (to age 100).
'Limited' refers to the premium payment period being limited to 30 years, not the duration of the coverage.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A limited pay T-100 does not need to be converted — it is already permanent (T-100). After the payment period, the policy is simply paid-up; A paid-up T-100 does not mature at the end of the payment period — coverage continues for life (to age 100).
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would be paid-up and she would retain the coverage.

questions.question:
Biff has an adjustable whole life policy. The policy offers a guaranteed premium for the first five years, but it can be adjusted by the insurer thereafter.
Which of the following variables could impact the adjustment?
optionFeedback: Administrative costs are one factor but not the only one.
Interest rates are one factor but not the only one.
Mortality rates are one factor but not the only one.
All of the above.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Administrative costs are one factor but not the only one; Interest rates are one factor but not the only one.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: All of the above.

questions.question: Archie purchased a whole life participating policy and selected the PUA dividend option. Which of the following statements is true regarding this PUA?
options: A. The PUA will not likely have a cash value.
B. The PUA will carry a term insurance addition option.
C. The PUA will require additional premiums.
D. The PUA is permanent insurance.
optionFeedback: Paid-up additions are whole life insurance and do accumulate their own cash value.
PUAs are paid-up whole life additions — they do not carry a separate term insurance option.
PUAs are 'paid-up' — no additional premiums are ever required for them.
The PUA is permanent insurance.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Paid-up additions are whole life insurance and do accumulate their own cash value; PUAs are paid-up whole life additions — they do not carry a separate term insurance option.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: The PUA is permanent insurance.

questions.question: Gracie purchased a participating whole life insurance policy and selected the PUA dividend option. She selected this option because it will result in increased coverage. What type of insurance is added?
optionFeedback: PUAs are not term riders — they are paid-up whole life additions that do not expire.
One-year term insurance is a separate dividend option — not the PUA option.
Whole life insurance.
PUAs are whole life insurance, not T-100. T-100 is a different permanent product.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. PUAs are not term riders — they are paid-up whole life additions that do not expire; One-year term insurance is a separate dividend option — not the PUA option.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Whole life insurance.

questions.question:
Larry suffered a severe back injury that required surgery. He had to take a leave of absence from work in order to properly recover after the surgery. He is likely to earn very little income while he is recovering.
Which of the following non-forfeiture options would best suit his needs?
optionFeedback: Surrendering the policy permanently ends coverage — not appropriate for a temporary financial hardship.
APL.
Extended term insurance converts the policy to a paid-up term plan; while it suspends premium payments, it is harder to reverse if Larry wants to resume original coverage later.
Reduced paid-up insurance permanently reduces the face amount — not ideal for a temporary situation.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Surrendering the policy permanently ends coverage — not appropriate for a temporary financial hardship; Extended term insurance converts the policy to a paid-up term plan; while it suspends premium payments, it is harder to reverse if Larry wants to resume original coverage later.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: APL.

questions.question:
Snap, Crackle, Pop, and Toucan are four friends who each bought different permanent insurance plans. They recently embarked on a once-in-a-lifetime 60-day trek to Africa, where they have no contact with anyone back home. Nearing the end of their trip, they all realize they forgot to make payments to their respective insurance policies.
All else being equal, which of the following policies is most likely to have lapsed?
optionFeedback: A limited payment T-100 that has completed its payment period requires no further premiums and cannot lapse due to missed payments.
A participating whole life policy accumulates cash value, which can fund the APL non-forfeiture option to prevent lapsing.
A non-participating whole life policy also accumulates cash value, which can fund the APL to prevent lapsing.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A limited payment T-100 that has completed its payment period requires no further premiums and cannot lapse due to missed payments; A participating whole life policy accumulates cash value, which can fund the APL non-forfeiture option to prevent lapsing.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: T-100.

questions.question:
Becky is an animal lover who spends much of her spare time volunteering at animal sanctuaries and is an activist for animal rights. She practices what she preaches and has adopted a vegan diet. She would like to purchase a permanent life insurance policy that will allow her to gift money to various charities upon her death.
Becky would like to pay the lowest possible premium, but there is one caveat; she acknowledges that she is very young and her life goals may change. As a result, there is a possibility that she may surrender the policy at some point down the road. She would also like to keep the premiums as low as possible.
Which of the following policies best meets her needs?
optionFeedback: Whole life insurance provides a CSV upon surrender but carries a higher premium than universal life for the same basic coverage.
10-year term insurance is not permanent — it would not allow her to gift money to charities upon death at an unknown future date.
Universal life insurance.
T-100 has the lowest premium among permanent policies, but it typically has no cash surrender value — if Becky surrenders it, she receives nothing.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Whole life insurance provides a CSV upon surrender but carries a higher premium than universal life for the same basic coverage; 10-year term insurance is not permanent — it would not allow her to gift money to charities upon death at an unknown future date.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Universal life insurance.
